bytes.topic: Framework Choice — PyTorch vs TensorFlow vs JAX, when each makes sense
bytes.title: TensorFlow vs PyTorch vs JAX — Choosing the Right Framework for Your ML Workflow
bytes.body:
TensorFlow's graph execution model via `tf.function` gives it a decisive edge in production: frozen graphs export cleanly to TensorFlow Lite, TensorFlow.js, and TensorFlow Serving without runtime dependencies. If you're deploying to mobile, edge devices, or a low-latency REST endpoint, TensorFlow's `SavedModel` format and `tf.saved_model.save()` pipeline are battle-tested in ways PyTorch's `torch.export` still isn't at scale.

PyTorch wins for research iteration — eager-by-default means fewer surprises and easier debugging with standard Python tooling. But if your team is already in TensorFlow and using `tf.data` pipelines with `tf.io.TFRecordDataset`, switching introduces real migration cost with little research-phase gain.

JAX is the right pick when you need composable function transforms (`jit`, `grad`, `vmap`, `pmap`) and are writing novel optimizers or custom training loops from scratch. It's not a framework — it's a numerical computing library with autograd.

Bottom line: TensorFlow for production serving and edge deployment, PyTorch for research flexibility, JAX for algorithm research requiring explicit control over vectorization and parallelism.

bytes.topic: Tensor Operations — fundamentals, broadcasting, device management (CPU/GPU)
bytes.title: TensorFlow Tensor Ops — Broadcasting, Shapes, and GPU Placement
bytes.body:
TensorFlow tensors are immutable, typed N-dimensional arrays. Every operation produces a new tensor — never mutates in place. Broadcasting follows NumPy semantics: dimensions are aligned from the right, and size-1 dims expand to match. A shape `[3,1]` tensor added to `[1,4]` yields `[3,4]` without materializing copies.

Device placement is explicit via `tf.device`. By default, TensorFlow greedily places ops on the first available GPU. Use `tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')` to inspect available devices, and wrap ops in `with tf.device('/GPU:0'):` to pin execution. Cross-device tensor copies are implicit but expensive — minimize them by keeping entire forward passes on a single device.

`tf.function` compiles a Python function into a static graph via tracing, which unlocks XLA fusion and eliminates Python overhead. The first call traces; subsequent calls reuse the graph. Avoid Python side effects inside `tf.function` — they run only on trace, not on every call. Use `tf.print` instead of `print` for in-graph logging.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

# Broadcasting: [3,1] + [1,4] => [3,4]
a = tf.constant([[1.0], [2.0], [3.0]])   # shape [3,1]
b = tf.constant([[10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0]])  # shape [1,4]
c = a + b  # shape [3,4], no copies allocated

# Explicit GPU placement
with tf.device('/GPU:0'):
    x = tf.random.normal([1024, 1024])
    y = tf.matmul(x, tf.transpose(x))

# Compiled graph via tf.function
@tf.function
def normalize(t: tf.Tensor) -> tf.Tensor:
    mean, variance = tf.nn.moments(t, axes=[0])
    return tf.nn.batch_normalization(t, mean, variance, None, None, 1e-8)

result = normalize(tf.random.normal([512, 128]))
tf.print("Output shape:", tf.shape(result))

bytes.topic: Model Architecture — layers, activation functions, building custom modules
bytes.title: TensorFlow Custom Layers — Extending tf.keras.layers.Layer the Right Way
bytes.body: TensorFlow's layer abstraction lives in `tf.keras.layers.Layer`. When built-in layers don't fit, subclass it directly — override `build()` to create weight tensors (called once, on first input shape) and `call()` for the forward pass. This separation matters: `build()` gives you `self.add_weight()` with proper initializer and regularizer support, while `call()` keeps computation clean and traceable by `tf.function`. Activation functions are just callables — pass `tf.nn.gelu` or a string like `'swish'` to `tf.keras.activations.get()` for runtime resolution, or compose them inline inside `call()`. For reusable blocks, nest layers as attributes in `__init__` so Keras tracks their weights automatically via `self._trainable_weights`. Skip manual weight registration — Keras walks your attributes. Mark non-training paths with `call(self, x, training=False)` and gate `tf.keras.layers.Dropout` or `BatchNormalization` behavior on that flag. Custom layers compose identically to built-ins inside `tf.keras.Sequential` or the Functional API, and they serialize cleanly if you implement `get_config()`.

bytes.topic: Training Loop — forward pass, loss computation, backpropagation, optimizer step
bytes.title: TensorFlow Training Loop — GradientTape, Loss, and Optimizer in One Pass
bytes.body: TensorFlow's training loop is explicit by design. Inside a `tf.GradientTape` context, every operation on watched variables is recorded to the computation graph. The forward pass runs your model, producing logits. Loss is computed immediately after — `tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(from_logits=True)` is the idiomatic choice for classification. Calling `tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)` then differentiates the loss with respect to each variable, traversing the recorded graph in reverse. The resulting gradient list is passed to `optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))` — this is your weight update step. Critically, `tape` is consumed after one `gradient()` call; set `persistent=True` only if you need multiple gradient computations from the same forward pass. Wrapping the entire loop body in `@tf.function` compiles it to a static graph via XLA, often delivering 2–4× throughput gains over eager mode. Always benchmark with and without `@tf.function` — shape dynamism can inhibit tracing.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

model = tf.keras.Sequential([tf.keras.layers.Dense(10)])
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=1e-3)
loss_fn = tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(from_logits=True)

@tf.function
def train_step(x, y):
    with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
        logits = model(x, training=True)
        loss = loss_fn(y, logits)
    grads = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
    optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
    return loss

bytes.topic: Optimizers — SGD, Adam, AdamW, learning rate schedulers
bytes.title: TensorFlow Optimizers — SGD, Adam, AdamW, and Learning Rate Schedulers
bytes.body: TensorFlow's `tf.keras.optimizers` gives you fine-grained control over training dynamics. SGD with momentum (`tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(lr=0.01, momentum=0.9, nesterov=True)`) is still the gold standard for CNNs on well-tuned pipelines. Adam (`tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=1e-3)`) adapts per-parameter learning rates using first and second moment estimates — fast convergence, but can generalize worse than SGD. AdamW (`tf.keras.optimizers.AdamW(weight_decay=1e-2)`) decouples weight decay from the gradient update, fixing Adam's L2 regularization conflation — use it for transformers and modern architectures. Pair any optimizer with a `tf.keras.optimizers.schedules` policy: `CosineDecay` for smooth annealing, `ExponentialDecay` for aggressive step-downs, or `ReduceLROnPlateau` via the callback equivalent. Wrap your schedule into the optimizer directly via the `learning_rate` argument — TensorFlow will call `schedule(step)` automatically during `optimizer.apply_gradients()`. For mixed-precision training, wrap with `tf.keras.mixed_precision.LossScaleOptimizer` to prevent underflow.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

# Cosine decay schedule
schedule = tf.keras.optimizers.schedules.CosineDecay(
    initial_learning_rate=1e-3,
    decay_steps=10000,
    alpha=1e-5  # minimum lr floor
)

# AdamW with decoupled weight decay + schedule
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.AdamW(
    learning_rate=schedule,
    weight_decay=1e-2
)

# Mixed precision wrapper for GPU/TPU
optimizer = tf.keras.mixed_precision.LossScaleOptimizer(optimizer)

bytes.topic: Loss Functions — choosing the right loss, custom loss functions
bytes.title: TensorFlow Loss Functions — Picking Right and Writing Your Own
bytes.body:
TensorFlow's `tf.keras.losses` covers most supervised learning needs, but the wrong choice silently kills training. Use `SparseCategoricalCrossentropy` when your labels are integers, `CategoricalCrossentropy` when they're one-hot — mixing them is a silent bug that won't raise an error. Set `from_logits=True` whenever your final layer has no activation; skipping this causes numerically unstable log computations. For regression, prefer `Huber` over `MeanSquaredError` when outliers are present — it's quadratic near zero, linear beyond a delta threshold.

When built-ins don't fit — say, a focal loss for class imbalance or a custom ranking objective — subclass `tf.keras.losses.Loss`. Override `call(y_true, y_pred)` and return a per-sample tensor; Keras handles reduction automatically based on the `reduction` argument you pass to `super().__init__()`. Avoid plain Python ops inside `call` — use `tf.math` equivalents so the function stays inside the graph and supports `tf.function` tracing. Always test your custom loss with `tf.debugging.assert_near` against a NumPy reference before plugging it into `model.compile`.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

class FocalLoss(tf.keras.losses.Loss):
    def __init__(self, gamma=2.0, alpha=0.25, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.alpha = alpha

    def call(self, y_true, y_pred):
        bce = tf.keras.losses.binary_crossentropy(
            y_true, y_pred, from_logits=False
        )
        p_t = y_true * y_pred + (1 - y_true) * (1 - y_pred)
        focal_weight = self.alpha * tf.pow(1.0 - p_t, self.gamma)
        return focal_weight * bce

model.compile(optimizer="adam", loss=FocalLoss(gamma=2.0))

bytes.topic: Data Loading — DataLoader, batching, shuffling, prefetching, augmentation
bytes.title: TensorFlow Data Pipelines — Batching, Shuffling, Prefetching with tf.data
bytes.body:
tf.data.Dataset is TensorFlow's high-performance input pipeline API. The pattern matters: shuffle before batch, not after — shuffling post-batch only reorders batches, not individual samples, leading to poor randomization.

Use `dataset.cache()` after expensive preprocessing (e.g., image decoding) but before `shuffle()` to avoid reprocessing on each epoch. Set `shuffle(buffer_size)` to at least your dataset size for true randomness; for large datasets, a buffer of 1000–10000 is a practical tradeoff.

`prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE)` overlaps data preprocessing and model execution — GPU sits idle without it. Place it last in your pipeline chain.

For augmentation, use `map()` with `tf.image` ops and pass `num_parallel_calls=tf.data.AUTOTUNE` to parallelize across CPU cores. Avoid Python-side augmentation (e.g., raw NumPy in `map()`) — it breaks graph execution and kills throughput.

`repeat()` is optional when using Keras `fit()` with `epochs` set; adding it anyway can cause silent infinite loops if epoch boundaries aren't handled.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

AUTOTUNE = tf.data.AUTOTUNE

def augment(image, label):
    image = tf.image.random_flip_left_right(image)
    image = tf.image.random_brightness(image, max_delta=0.1)
    return image, label

dataset = (
    tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices((images, labels))
    .cache()
    .shuffle(buffer_size=1000, seed=42, reshuffle_each_iteration=True)
    .map(augment, num_parallel_calls=AUTOTUNE)
    .batch(32, drop_remainder=True)
    .prefetch(AUTOTUNE)
)

bytes.topic: Regularization — dropout, weight decay, batch normalization, early stopping
bytes.title: TensorFlow Regularization — Dropout, Weight Decay, BatchNorm, and Early Stopping
bytes.body: TensorFlow offers four primary regularization mechanisms, each targeting a different failure mode. `tf.keras.layers.Dropout(rate)` randomly zeros activations during training — wrap it after dense or conv layers, never during inference (Keras handles this via the `training` flag automatically). Weight decay is applied per-layer via `kernel_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L2(1e-4)` — this adds a penalty term directly to the loss, so you'll see it reflected in `model.losses`. `tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()` normalizes activations per mini-batch and learns scale/shift parameters (`gamma`, `beta`); place it before the activation, not after. Critically, BatchNorm has separate train/inference behavior — always pass `training=True` explicitly in custom training loops, or it defaults to inference mode and your model won't learn. For early stopping, `tf.keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', patience=5, restore_best_weights=True)` halts training when validation loss stalls and resets weights to the best checkpoint. Combine all four: BatchNorm reduces need for aggressive dropout; L2 and dropout together can over-regularize — tune one at a time.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(256, kernel_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L2(1e-4)),
    tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization(),
    tf.keras.layers.Activation('relu'),
    tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.3),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])

early_stop = tf.keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
    monitor='val_loss', patience=5, restore_best_weights=True
)

model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_split=0.2, epochs=100, callbacks=[early_stop])

bytes.topic: Transfer Learning — pretrained models, fine-tuning vs feature extraction, frozen layers
bytes.title: TensorFlow Transfer Learning — Fine-Tuning vs Feature Extraction with MobileNetV2
bytes.body:
Transfer learning in TensorFlow lets you leverage pretrained weights from models like MobileNetV2, EfficientNet, or ResNet trained on ImageNet. The strategy you choose determines both training speed and accuracy ceiling.

**Feature extraction** freezes the entire base model (`base_model.trainable = False`) and only trains a new classification head. Fast, low risk of overfitting, ideal when your dataset is small or similar to ImageNet.

**Fine-tuning** unfreezes the top layers of the base model and retrains them alongside your head at a very low learning rate (1e-5 range). This adapts high-level feature detectors to your domain — critical when your data differs significantly from ImageNet.

A common two-phase approach: first run feature extraction for ~10 epochs to warm up the head, then unfreeze the top N layers of the base and fine-tune. Unfreezing too early collapses pretrained weights. Use `base_model.layers[-20:]` to target only the final blocks. Always recompile after changing `trainable` — TensorFlow rebuilds the computation graph on `model.compile()`.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

base_model = tf.keras.applications.MobileNetV2(input_shape=(224, 224, 3), include_top=False, weights='imagenet')
base_model.trainable = False  # Phase 1: feature extraction

model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    base_model,
    tf.keras.layers.GlobalAveragePooling2D(),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(train_ds, epochs=10)

# Phase 2: fine-tune top 20 layers
base_model.trainable = True
for layer in base_model.layers[:-20]:
    layer.trainable = False
model.compile(optimizer=tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(1e-5), loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])
model.fit(train_ds, epochs=5)

bytes.topic: GPU Acceleration — CUDA, moving tensors to device, mixed precision training
bytes.title: TensorFlow GPU Acceleration — CUDA Devices, Tensor Placement & Mixed Precision
bytes.body:
TensorFlow automatically places ops on the first available GPU, but explicit placement gives you control. Use `tf.device('/GPU:0')` context managers to force tensor creation and computation onto a specific CUDA device. Verify GPU visibility with `tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')` — if empty, your CUDA/cuDNN versions don't match the TensorFlow build.

For mixed precision training, enable the `mixed_float16` global policy via `tf.keras.mixed_precision.set_global_policy('mixed_float16')`. This stores weights in float32 but casts compute to float16, cutting VRAM usage roughly in half and boosting throughput on Ampere+ GPUs via Tensor Cores. One critical detail: wrap your optimizer with `LossScaleOptimizer` to prevent float16 gradient underflow — Keras does this automatically when the policy is set before model construction.

Use `tf.config.experimental.set_memory_growth(gpu, True)` to prevent TensorFlow from pre-allocating all GPU memory, which is essential when sharing a device across processes or Jupyter sessions.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

# Confirm GPU availability
gpus = tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')
for gpu in gpus:
    tf.config.experimental.set_memory_growth(gpu, True)

# Enable mixed precision globally (before model construction)
tf.keras.mixed_precision.set_global_policy('mixed_float16')

# Explicit device placement
with tf.device('/GPU:0'):
    x = tf.constant([[1.0, 2.0], [3.0, 4.0]])  # created on GPU:0
    y = tf.matmul(x, x)

# Build model — optimizer auto-wrapped with LossScaleOptimizer
model = tf.keras.Sequential([tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu')])
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='mse')

bytes.topic: Debugging Models — gradient flow, NaN loss, exploding/vanishing gradients
bytes.title: TensorFlow Model Debugging — Gradient Flow, NaN Loss & Exploding/Vanishing Gradients
bytes.body: NaN loss almost always traces back to three sources: exploding gradients, a bad learning rate, or log(0) in your loss function. Start with `tf.debugging.check_numerics()` — wrap suspicious tensors to pinpoint the exact op where NaN first appears. For exploding gradients, apply gradient clipping via `optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(clipnorm=1.0)` or manually clip with `tf.clip_by_global_norm(grads, 1.0)` before applying. Vanishing gradients are subtler — use `tf.GradientTape` with `tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)` and log gradient norms per layer. If early layers show near-zero norms, switch activations from `sigmoid` to `relu` or `swish`, or add batch normalization. TensorBoard's histogram dashboard (`tf.summary.histogram`) is your best visualization tool — log weight and gradient distributions each epoch to catch divergence early. For custom training loops, `tf.debugging.assert_all_finite(loss, 'Loss has NaN/Inf')` will hard-fail before corrupt gradients propagate through the graph.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=1e-3, clipnorm=1.0)

@tf.function
def train_step(x, y):
    with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
        logits = model(x, training=True)
        loss = loss_fn(y, logits)
        tf.debugging.assert_all_finite(loss, 'NaN/Inf loss detected')

    grads = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
    grads, global_norm = tf.clip_by_global_norm(grads, 1.0)

    for i, (g, v) in enumerate(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables)):
        tf.summary.histogram(f'grad/{v.name}', g, step=optimizer.iterations)

    optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
    return loss

bytes.topic: Model Evaluation — metrics per task type, confusion matrix, cross-validation
bytes.title: TensorFlow Model Evaluation — Metrics, Confusion Matrices, and Cross-Validation
bytes.body:
Choosing the right evaluation metric in TensorFlow depends on task type. For binary classification, use `tf.keras.metrics.AUC`, `Precision`, and `Recall` — accuracy alone is misleading on imbalanced datasets. For multiclass, `SparseCategoricalAccuracy` handles integer labels directly without one-hot encoding. For regression, `MeanAbsoluteError` and `RootMeanSquaredError` are built-in and composable.

Confusion matrices aren't native to Keras metrics but integrate cleanly via `tensorflow_model_analysis` or scikit-learn after extracting predictions with `model.predict()`. Use `tf.math.confusion_matrix(labels, predictions)` for a pure-TF tensor result you can log to TensorBoard.

Cross-validation isn't built into Keras, but you can wrap any `tf.keras.Model` with `scikeras.wrappers.KerasClassifier` to use `sklearn.model_selection.StratifiedKFold`. For large datasets, prefer manual fold loops using `tf.data.Dataset.shard()` to keep the pipeline GPU-efficient. Always reset model weights between folds with `model.set_weights(initial_weights)` to prevent state leakage.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
import numpy as np

model = tf.keras.Sequential([tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid', input_shape=(20,))])
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='binary_crossentropy',
              metrics=[tf.keras.metrics.AUC(name='auc'), tf.keras.metrics.Precision(), tf.keras.metrics.Recall()])

initial_weights = model.get_weights()
kf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for fold, (train_idx, val_idx) in enumerate(kf.split(X, y)):
    model.set_weights(initial_weights)  # reset between folds
    model.fit(X[train_idx], y[train_idx], epochs=10, verbose=0)
    results = model.evaluate(X[val_idx], y[val_idx], verbose=0)
    print(f"Fold {fold+1} — AUC: {results[1]:.4f}, Precision: {results[2]:.4f}, Recall: {results[3]:.4f}")

cm = tf.math.confusion_matrix(y_true, tf.round(model.predict(X_test)))
print(cm)

bytes.topic: scikit-learn — pipeline API, preprocessing, cross-validation, model selection
bytes.title: TensorFlow + scikit-learn — Plug Keras Models into sklearn Pipelines with SciKeras
bytes.body:
TensorFlow users often rebuild preprocessing and cross-validation logic from scratch when sklearn already solves it. SciKeras (`scikeras.wrappers.KerasClassifier / KerasRegressor`) wraps any `tf.keras` model into a fully sklearn-compatible estimator, unlocking `Pipeline`, `GridSearchCV`, and `cross_val_score` without touching your model architecture.

Wrap your model factory in `KerasClassifier`, then slot it as the final step in a `Pipeline`. Upstream steps — `StandardScaler`, `ColumnTransformer`, custom transformers — run automatically before the Keras forward pass during both fit and predict. Hyperparameters prefixed with `model__` (e.g. `model__hidden_units`) flow through to your factory function, so `GridSearchCV` tunes architecture and learning rate alongside preprocessing choices in a single search.

Cross-validation via `cv=StratifiedKFold(5)` respects class balance and refits on every fold with proper weight initialization — no state leaks between folds. `gs.best_estimator_` returns the full pipeline, including fitted scaler, ready for `joblib.dump`. This pattern keeps TensorFlow's flexibility while leveraging sklearn's battle-tested model selection infrastructure.
bytes.code_snippet:
from scikeras.wrappers import KerasClassifier
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, StratifiedKFold
import tensorflow as tf

def build_model(hidden_units=64, learning_rate=1e-3):
    model = tf.keras.Sequential([
        tf.keras.layers.Dense(hidden_units, activation="relu"),
        tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation="sigmoid"),
    ])
    model.compile(
        optimizer=tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate),
        loss="binary_crossentropy", metrics=["accuracy"]
    )
    return model

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
    ("model", KerasClassifier(model=build_model, epochs=30, verbose=0)),
])

param_grid = {
    "model__hidden_units": [32, 64],
    "model__learning_rate": [1e-3, 1e-4],
}
gs = GridSearchCV(pipe, param_grid, cv=StratifiedKFold(3), scoring="accuracy", n_jobs=-1)
gs.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(gs.best_params_, gs.best_score_)

bytes.topic: XGBoost — gradient boosting, hyperparameter tuning, feature importance
bytes.title: TensorFlow Decision Forests — Gradient Boosting, Hyperparameter Tuning, and Feature Importance
bytes.body:
TensorFlow Decision Forests (TF-DF) brings gradient boosted trees into the Keras ecosystem — no scikit-learn bridge required. `GradientBoostedTreesModel` trains directly on `tf.data.Dataset` pipelines, handling categorical features natively without manual encoding.

The critical hyperparameters mirror XGBoost semantics but use TF-DF naming: `shrinkage` is the learning rate, `subsample` controls row sampling, and `max_depth` caps tree complexity. Rather than manual grid search, TF-DF ships `HyperParameterTemplate` presets — `"benchmark_rank1"` applies competition-proven defaults tuned on 100+ OpenML datasets, cutting cold-start tuning time significantly.

Feature importance is exposed through `model.make_inspector()`, returning multiple importance types: `NUM_AS_ROOT` (how often a feature splits at the root — proxy for global dominance), `SUM_SCORE` (total score gain, analogous to XGBoost's `gain`), and `NUM_WEIGHTED_AS_ROOT`. All are computed during training with zero post-hoc overhead.

For production, `model.save()` produces a standard TF SavedModel — the same artifact served via TF Serving or deployed to Vertex AI, keeping your inference infrastructure uniform across neural and tree-based models.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow_decision_forests as tfdf

train_ds = tfdf.keras.pd_dataframe_to_tf_dataset(train_df, label="target")
val_ds   = tfdf.keras.pd_dataframe_to_tf_dataset(val_df,   label="target")

model = tfdf.keras.GradientBoostedTreesModel(
    num_trees=300,
    max_depth=6,
    shrinkage=0.05,
    subsample=0.8,
    tuner=tfdf.keras.HyperParameterTemplate("benchmark_rank1"),
)
model.fit(train_ds, validation_data=val_ds)

inspector = model.make_inspector()
for feat in inspector.variable_importances()["SUM_SCORE"]:
    print(f"{feat.name}: {feat.importance:.4f}")

bytes.topic: JAX — functional transformations (jit, grad, vmap), XLA compilation
bytes.title: TensorFlow's JAX-Style Stack: tf.function + XLA, GradientTape, and vectorized_map
bytes.body:
TensorFlow's answer to JAX's functional trio lives in `tf.function(jit_compile=True)` for XLA-fused kernel compilation, `tf.GradientTape` for explicit gradient control, and `tf.vectorized_map` for vmap-style batched parallelism — all composable within eager mode ergonomics.

Decorating a function with `@tf.function(jit_compile=True)` traces it to HLO IR and hands it to XLA, which fuses ops, eliminates temporary buffers, and typically yields 2–5× throughput gains on TPUs and modern GPUs. Unlike JAX's `jit`, TF's version handles stateful `tf.Variable` updates, but will retrace on input shape changes unless `input_signature` is explicitly set — an expensive footgun in production pipelines.

`tf.GradientTape` gives you imperative, explicit gradient computation. Nest tapes for higher-order derivatives, or call `tape.jacobian()` for per-sample Jacobians without a Python loop. For batched gradient computation, `tf.vectorized_map` maps a single-example function across the batch axis — TF's functional `vmap` equivalent — emitting one fused vectorized kernel instead of N sequential calls. Compose all three for efficient, TPU-ready training loops.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

@tf.function(jit_compile=True)  # XLA: fuses ops, eliminates temp buffers
def forward(x, w):
    return tf.nn.relu(x @ w)

x = tf.random.normal([256, 64])
w = tf.Variable(tf.random.normal([64, 128]))

# Explicit gradient control via GradientTape
with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
    out = forward(x, w)
    loss = tf.reduce_mean(out ** 2)

grad = tape.gradient(loss, w)  # dL/dw

# vmap-style batching: single-example fn vectorized over batch axis
per_sample_out = tf.vectorized_map(lambda xi: forward(xi[None], w), x)
# shape: [256, 1, 128] — one fused kernel, no Python loop

bytes.topic: Keras — high-level API, functional vs sequential, custom layers and training loops
bytes.title: TensorFlow Keras — Functional API, Custom Layers, and Training Loops
bytes.body:
Keras in TensorFlow 2.x ships three model-building styles. Sequential is fine for straight pipelines, but the Functional API unlocks shared layers, multi-input/output graphs, and residual connections — you wire tensors explicitly, giving you a DAG rather than a stack. For anything Sequential can't express, subclass `tf.keras.Model` and define `call()` yourself.

Custom layers live in `tf.keras.layers.Layer`. Override `build()` to create weights lazily (shape-dependent init) and `call()` for the forward pass. Mark weights with `self.add_weight()` so Keras tracks them for gradient updates and serialization.

When `model.fit()` is too opaque — custom gradient clipping, auxiliary losses, multi-optimizer steps — drop into a manual training loop using `tf.GradientTape`. Call the model inside the tape context, compute your loss, then apply `optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))`. Pair this with `@tf.function` to JIT-compile the step for near-graph performance without abandoning Python control flow.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

class ScaledDense(tf.keras.layers.Layer):
    def build(self, input_shape):
        self.w = self.add_weight(shape=(input_shape[-1], 64), initializer='glorot_uniform', trainable=True)
        self.scale = self.add_weight(shape=(), initializer='ones', trainable=True)

    def call(self, x):
        return tf.matmul(x, self.w) * self.scale

# Functional API model
inputs = tf.keras.Input(shape=(128,))
x = ScaledDense()(inputs)
outputs = tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')(x)
model = tf.keras.Model(inputs, outputs)

optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam()
loss_fn = tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy()

@tf.function
def train_step(x_batch, y_batch):
    with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
        preds = model(x_batch, training=True)
        loss = loss_fn(y_batch, preds)
    grads = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
    optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
    return loss

bytes.topic: Saving & Loading — checkpointing, model serialization, format choices
bytes.title: TensorFlow Model Serialization — SavedModel vs. Keras H5 vs. Weights-Only
bytes.body:
TensorFlow offers three primary serialization strategies, each with distinct trade-offs. The `SavedModel` format (default since TF 2.0) is the production standard — it exports the full computation graph, variables, and serving signatures, making it portable across TF Serving, TF Lite, and TF.js without needing the original Python class definition. Use `model.save('path/to/dir')` to produce it.

Keras `.h5` is legacy but still common for research. It bundles architecture + weights in one file, but struggles with custom layers and non-sequential topologies. Avoid it for anything you'll deploy.

For training resumption, checkpointing with `tf.train.Checkpoint` is more granular than full serialization — it saves optimizer state, step count, and arbitrary variables. Pair it with `tf.train.CheckpointManager` to retain only the last N checkpoints and auto-restore the latest.

Weights-only serialization via `model.save_weights()` / `model.load_weights()` is fastest for fine-tuning workflows where architecture is fixed in code. Use `save_format='tf'` (default) over `'h5'` for checkpoint-style sharding on large models.
bytes.code_snippet:
import tensorflow as tf

model = tf.keras.Sequential([tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'), tf.keras.layers.Dense(10)])
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy')

# SavedModel (recommended for serving)
model.save('saved_model/my_model')
restored = tf.keras.models.load_model('saved_model/my_model')

# Checkpoint with manager (recommended for training)
ckpt = tf.train.Checkpoint(model=model, optimizer=model.optimizer)
manager = tf.train.CheckpointManager(ckpt, './ckpts', max_to_keep=3)
manager.save()
ckpt.restore(manager.latest_checkpoint).expect_partial()

bytes.topic: Production Readiness — model export, ONNX conversion, serving considerations
bytes.title: TensorFlow Production Readiness — SavedModel, ONNX Export, and TF Serving
bytes.body:
When shipping a TensorFlow model to production, the export format is your first decision. `tf.saved_model.save()` produces a SavedModel directory — the canonical format for TF Serving and TFLite. Always trace your model with concrete input signatures via `@tf.function(input_signature=[...])` before export; without it, TF Serving can't infer the serving signature and defaults to dynamic dispatch, which breaks strict type validation.

For cross-framework serving (e.g., deploying to an ONNX Runtime backend), use `tf2onnx`: `python -m tf2onnx.convert --saved-model ./model --output model.onnx --opset 17`. Opset 17 covers most TF ops, but custom `tf.keras` layers with Python-side logic (like dynamic masking) won't trace cleanly — rewrite them as pure TF ops first.

For TF Serving specifically, version your exports under a numeric subdirectory (`1/`, `2/`) — the server hot-reloads the highest integer. Warm up your model with `WarmupRequests` in `assets.extra/` to avoid cold-start latency spikes on the first batch.
